text: So I'm presenting a sixty-seven-year-old female, former housewife, with the following known diseases. Arterial hypertension, diabetes, she performed a thyroidectomy in the past, and also a carpal syndrome surgery. She is currently medicated with glicazide, metformin, levothyroxine, olmezartan, simvastatin, and ethyl loflacepate.

In January of this current year, she had her first episode of macroscopic hematuria. She then performed a cystoscopy that showed a four to five centimeter right wall bladder tumor, and she went through in April of this year to a TURB that fully resected the tumor. In May, her histology was positive for a muscle invasive bladder tumor with no positive lymph nodes and no metastasis.
The video displays a thickened bladder wall near the retral meatus on the left side of the screen, while the right side shows a thinning of the renal parenchyma with visible retrohydronephrosis.

Dr. Peter Viklund will be performing a robotic radical cystectomy, followed by an extended lymphonectomy, and afterwards, Dr. Inderberg-Gill will perform intercorporeal orthotropic neobladder. Okay, so welcome. I don't know if, do we still have the external camera? So welcome to the OR-One today, where we're going to do a cystectomy.

We started a little bit, I will show you in a minute, but I first like Rodolfo Sanchez here, who is accompanying me from Karolinska, is assisting today. And I have a wonderful crew, also local here, that are helping me. And hopefully we can understand each other also. We speak Spanish. English, Portuguese and Swedish in here. So we will hope that we get everything right in the end. So maybe Rodolfo, you could show how the ports are. Can we have an external view of the ports? Because I think this is one of the crucial thing when you do cystectomy is that the ports are placed in the right position. So yes, Rodolfo, you can take over.

The surgical team positions the camera port three centimeters above the umbilicus. The right robotic port is placed eleven centimeters to the right at the same level, and the left robotic port ten centimeters to the left. An air seal port is positioned three centimeters above the anterior right spine, mirrored by a fifty millimeter port three centimeters above the left anterior spine using a port-in-port technique. The fifty millimeter port is used for specimen retrieval and stapling the posterior neobladder. An additional twelve millimeter port is placed between the right robotic port and the camera port for assistance.

So all in all, we have four robotics ports and two assistant ports. And one of the robotic ports where we have the fourth arm or the extra arm, we also go to use to put in the specimen bag and also to do the stapling of the intestine. Now we will see maybe it's in the gill who will do the stapling in the end. So we will see. But that's how everything is set up here.

The surgeon has completed dissection of the right ureter, revealing two dilated ureters due to advanced tumor involvement. The ureters are parallel on the right side, and a clip is placed near the bladder. The surgical team is now beginning dissection on the left side, starting with the sigmoid colon.

The tumor, as you heard, is located on the right side mostly. And relatively close to the meatus, we have to be careful for the cystectomy so that we don't get too close to the tumor, especially since this patient is scheduled for a neobladder. It's not ideal to have patients with tumor close to the bladder neck in women if you want to do neobladders, of course. So we have to be very careful there.

The surgeon locates the ureter on the left side, noting its smaller and more normal appearance compared to the right side. The dissection begins on the right side due to the sigmoid colon's attachment to the left, using a grasper to pull the intestine over to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon carefully navigates near the left ureter, aiming to approach close to the bladder while avoiding damage to the ureter's circulation. The internal and external common iliac arteries are visible in the surgical field, with plans to return for lymph node dissection. The left ureter appears smaller due to obstruction observed on the right side.

The surgeon navigates around the more lateral female bladder, exercising caution due to anatomical differences compared to the male bladder. The surgical field reveals the broad ligament, which is being opened to expose the ureter that has been released down to this area.

Peter, you talked about chemotherapy. Yes. Can you give us some clues about the indication at your institution? Yes. Why should this patient... Yes, so in Karolinska, we give... I mean, according to the... the trials that have been done. There is not a huge, but there is a survival benefit from patients that have neoadjuvant chemotherapy if they have a muscle invasive tumor. So we try to give all our patients with muscle invasive cancer, new advent chemotherapy. And in fact, we have a downstaging to PT zero in almost fifty percent of the cases. And in the group where you have the downstaging, the oncological outcome of this operation is excellent. Can you come with a clip here?
The surgeon clips the left ureter, using a clip with a suture to facilitate lifting. The artery is visible nearby as the procedure continues.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the left and right ureters and is preparing to begin the cystectomy. The procedure will not be organ-sparing, as the uterus will be removed.

The surgeon identifies the anatomical landmarks, including the ureters and bladder, while preparing to make an incision in the vaginal area for orientation during the cystectomy. A sponge is visible in the surgical field, and suction is applied to reduce air inside the cavity.

The surgeon begins the cystectomy on the right side, using a ligature to secure the ovarian vessels. The bladder ureter is visible, along with a hole in the vagina. The surgeon proceeds to dissect the lateral pedicle, moving laterally to the umbilical ligament.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical ligament and moves structures laterally. A medial hernia is observed near the bone, which is noted as uncommon in females.

The surgeon examines the unusual hernia, revealing two ureters and the hernia itself. The dissection proceeds laterally towards the endopelvic fascia, while keeping the bladder attached at the top to prevent it from obstructing the surgical field. The pelvic bone and pelvic fascia become visible as the dissection progresses.
The obturator fossa is visible, with the obturator nerve identifiable within it. The nerve is clearly seen in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a ligature to divide the round ligament. The umbilical ligament is visible, and the surgeon follows it internally, demonstrating the anatomical structures.

The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery and the umbilical ligament, noting the presence of the superior vesical artery. Clips are applied to both the umbilical ligament and the superior vesical artery simultaneously, followed by the use of a ligature to secure these structures.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and applies a ligature to secure it, repeating the process to ensure proper placement.

The surgeon manipulates the uterus, elevating it within the surgical field. A ligature is applied as the dissection progresses towards the opening of the vagina. A sponge stick is visible, aiding in maintaining the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies large veins in the surgical field and applies a ligature to secure them, noting their potential relation to the patient's tumor. Clips are placed on the veins to prevent excessive bleeding. The surgeon then removes a small amount of fat tissue to improve visibility in the surgical area.

The surgeon applies clips to large veins, followed by the use of a ligature to secure the vessels. The dissection progresses towards the vagina, which is opened, and additional clips are applied to secure the area.

The surgeon identifies the vein and the lateral side of the vagina, avoiding proximity to the bladder due to the tumor location. The surgical approach is more radical, following the lateral side of the vagina for oncological reasons.

And maybe you can go with your ligature. You don't have to cut, actually. You can just close it and then... Yes. Yes. Because you want to spare as much of the anterior wall as possible when you do a neobladder. Here we have the... Okay, I think we'll leave it like this and we will take the rest. Peter, may I ask you a question? Sometimes do you spare the vagina completely or never? I do spare the vagina completely. I think that the functional outcome is better. If you can spare the vagina, I would like to try to do it. But in this particular case, her tumor is right here. And I don't want to spare that because for oncological reasons, it will be very dangerous in this specific case. But I do think that for neobladders, because I think that the outcome for neobladders in females is not as good functionally as in males. So I want to spare as much as possible because I think the functional outcome becomes much better if you can spare the vagina. What is the main risk in terms of continence? Hyper continence or incontinence? In my hands, I see more incontinence than hyper continence. I don't know exactly why that is, but we have more problems.

Something like fifty percent of the females in our series will have problems with incontinence rather than hypercontinence And I try to spare as much as possible Of the urethra and bladder neck if I can so so I don't really understand this but that's how it is but and I think of course that the important to spare as much of the nerves as possible on the lateral side so in the case like this when you have your tumor in this region and this also becomes a little bit complicated. You can go with your ligature here. I think that I will try to do a little bit nicer sparing here on the left side. If I come in the right plane, I will try to do that. So you try to spare the pudental nerves, that's right? Yes, exactly, exactly. Thank you.
The surgeon proceeds laterally to the umbilical ligament on the left side, aiming for a better approach for tumor removal. The anatomical structures visible include the bone and the bladder.

The surgeon follows the umbilical ligament, revealing the obturator fossa, obturator nerve, and obturator artery. The dissection progresses on this side, with attention to the position of the pudendal nerves.

The surgeon applies pressure to the pudendal nerves on the lateral side, aiming to preserve them during dissection. The umbilical ligaments are visible, and the first artery is identified for further surgical manipulation using a ligature.
The surgeon identifies the veins and applies a clip, followed by the use of a ligature to secure the vessels.

The surgeon identifies the uterus and vagina, with the ureter visible nearby. Using a ligature, they avoid the veins and focus on preserving the important nerves that run laterally into the vagina. The surgeon adjusts the position using their fourth arm to improve visibility of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon places a clip on a structure near the bladder, noting that the bladder sits more laterally in female anatomy than expected. Care is taken to avoid entering a plane too close to the bladder during dissection.

The surgeon carefully lifts the bladder, revealing the underlying vagina and sparing the neurovascular bundle, which is positioned similarly to the male prostate. The bladder is elevated to avoid descending too far on the side, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid opening the vagina on the left side, lifting structures to spare important nerves. The visual shows the difference in approach between the right side, where dissection is deeper, and the left side, where the surgeon follows the lateral surface of the vagina.

The surgeon identifies the plane on the side to move the vagina down, using a ligature to manipulate the tissue. Instead of moving down, the structures are lifted closer to the midline, deviating from the typical approach in female cystectomies.

The surgeon takes the bladder down and performs a urethral dissection, aiming to spare enough tissue for patient satisfaction. The urachus is divided, and the peritoneum is opened. The anatomy on the left side is more standard compared to the unusual right side. The catheter is carefully pulled to visualize the urethra and bladder neck.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the catheter balloon, pulling it down to reveal its position near the bladder neck. On the left side, the balloon is visible, while on the right side, a tumor is observed close to the bladder neck, indicating the proximity of the tumor to the surgical site.

I rely more on my frozen sections than on my... preoperative or I mean if you have biopsies during surgery beforehand the TRB but depends on your pathologist and so on if you really have a good pathologist who you can rely on or yes so you what's your rate of reliability is about ninety five percent and one hundred percent how much Yes. I mean, so it depends. For me, it depends a lot on where you are and who is doing the pathology, basically. But I do think that the preoperative pathology in our institution is not completely reliable.

The surgeon adjusts the catheter to visualize the urethra, pulling it down to better position it below the tumor, closer to the midline. This maneuver improves the surgical field for further dissection.

When we compare now our time in Karolinska with the Swedish National Register for cystectomy, the robotic cystectomies are exactly the same time as what's done open in Sweden.

Actually, we have fewer long cases in Karolinska than we'll find in Sweden in general. Do we have some unexpected recurrence or for you it's nothing wrong? You mean oncological? Well, I mean, we have now data from the, not the Swedish, but from the consortium of robotic surgeons in Europe, the Aero Scientific Working Group, and they look very nice, I must say. So there is no, I have no issues with oncological outcome. There is this debate about that Indy was discussing this morning, whether there is a different recurrence pattern and so on. But when you look into the literature, there is no evidence. We have no evidence of this in Europe in this scientific working groups data either. There are very, very few early recurrences and there is no really thing to suggest that this is different from what you find in Open surgery. I think that the robot is a tool to do surgery. It's not something that is going to change oncology very much. I really don't think so. I think it's more with the patient outcomes, which are important. Bring everything minimally invasive. What is your main hospital stay? What did you say again? Sorry, I couldn't hear. About the hospital stay. Sorry, I can't hear. There's so much noise going on here, so it's difficult to hear. Sorry about that. So the hospital stay now is seven days or seven nights. No, sorry, six nights, seven days. It depends on how you say it. Okay, yeah, okay. But that's much shorter than we have in our open series before. So we have been able to almost have to stay in the hospital since we went robotic. So we try to send the patient home on day six. That's what we aim to do. What is the day of rehabilitation? Is it when the patient passes the gas or...? You have to wait. No, so what we do now, you can take this just perfect. So the patient can start eating immediately post-operative. Okay. regardless whether they pass gas or not. But we do it a little bit carefully, so it's not like they have a big steak and fries, you know. But they will start eating slowly, but they will start already to drink and eat a little bit the first day, postoperative. And I think this is, I mean, this is for me the main benefit with robotic surgery.

I think that you can do exactly the same type of reservoir with the robot as you can do open. I don't think that you have to do any shortcuts. You can do the same spheric neobladder. You can do the same volume. You can do everything the same. It's just decide how much intestine you want to staple and how much you want to do. This, for me, is no issue. Why don't you start from the urethra? You start from the higher part. You mean start here and sit like this? Yeah, why? Because I want to end at this particular point. I don't mind if I end here or there or whatever. I will just end. But I want to be at this point. Because if I start at the point down there, I'm not sure that I'm going to end up where I want, basically. That's the reason why I start like this. Do you feel there is any advantage of having two teams, one for the cystectomy and one for the knee or bladder? If I have the advantage of doing the chimney or what? There's too many sounds in here again. Two different teams, one for the cystectomy and one for the knee or bladder. Can you please be quiet when they ask me questions because I cannot hear anything. Okay, please. Yes, once again, sorry. The question was if you feel there is any advantage of having two different teams, one for the cystectomy and another for the neobladder. Okay, if they're different teams. Yes, so in the beginning, I do think that it is an advantage of having two different teams. I do think that once you learn to do this in like three, four hours, then this advantage disappear. But I do think when you start, probably it's a good thing to have two teams. So you can because it's a tire. I mean, there's so many steps involved in this type of surgery. So it looks relatively easy if you see somebody who is used to do this.

The surgeon performs a final suture on the lower section of the surgical site, ensuring a neat closure. The team discusses the use of two teams for different parts of the procedure, with one team handling the cystectomy and lymph node removal, and another team performing the diversion.

What is the incidence of night incontinence with this kind of reservoir? I think it's exactly the same as in open surgery. I think that we had published something like sixty percent of night incontinence, but I think it has so much to do with the reservoir. It has how you coach your patient, basically. So it's the same with the daytime. So if you teach your patients to avoid at a regular basis, they go up at night, they put their alarm clock and so on, they will have a good chance of being incontinent. If they don't do that, they will have a very poor chance of being incontinent at night. So I think it has to do with how you coach your patients rather than the type of... But I have some people who doesn't want to get up at night to empty the bladder. If you don't go up at night and empty the bladder, you will have leakage problem. Even after some time, you will continue to leak. But that's the same thing when I did my open stoddor, so that hasn't changed basically. so i did open studio for many years before i started this so and i do think that this the actual neo bladder is very similar to open and the functional results regarding continence and so on are very similar i think what what we can do much better now is to do a nerve sparing cystectomy at least in my hands i have a much better erectile outcome

So I think the biggest advantages doing this is the short-time post-operative outcome and the erectile function outcome. Regarding the sexual function, with this kind of operation, do you have results better than in the open procedure? Yes, I have in my hands, but you know that the results from individual surgeons cannot always be translated. But I am better than to preserve potency when I do the robotic surgery than when I do open surgery. Yes. Aha. Can you cut the needle here? Oh, now it came. It came. It's fine.
